turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court in Bartenwerfer v. Buckley, number 21-908.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Petitioner Kate Bartenwerfer and her husband David remodeled the house with the goal of selling it at a profit. When they sold it to respondent Kieran Buckley, David and Kate failed to disclose material defects in the house. Buckley sued the Bartenwerfers in California State Court and want over $200,000 in damages. The Bartenwerfers then filed for Chapter 7 Bankruptcy. Buckley claimed that the bankruptcy codes fraud discharge exception precluded the Bartenwerfers from discharging the damages award. That was certainly true for David who had knowingly concealed the house's defects. The question is whether the exception also applies to Kate because the Bankruptcy Court found that she neither knew nor should have known of David's fraud. The Bankruptcy Appellate Panel held that Kate could discharge the debt because she was not the wrongdoer but the Ninth Circuit reversed holding that a debtor liable for her partner's fraud cannot discharge that debt regardless of her own culpability. We agree with the Ninth Circuit. Kate Bartenwerfer cannot discharge her debt to Buckley and bankruptcy. The fraud discharge exception applies to debts for money ?obtained by fraud?. Written in the passive voice, this exception focuses on how the money was obtained, not who committed the fraud to obtain it. It's true that the context of a passive voice sentence can make the relevant actor clear. Kate argues that in context the phrase obtained by fraud most naturally means obtained by the debtor's fraud. We disagree. The context of this statute works against Kate because the common law of fraud has long maintained that liability is not limited to the wrongdoer. Principals can be liable for the frauds of their agents for example, and individuals can be liable for the frauds of their partners. Our precedent and Congress' response to it confirm this understanding. Previously the fraud discharge exception applied only to debts created by the fraud ?of the bankrupt?. The very position that Bartenwerfer advocates here. Nonetheless in Strang v. Bradner, we held that two individuals could not discharge a debt created by the fraud of their business partner. Soon after Congress eliminated the words of the bankrupt from the statute and the implication is unmistakable, Congress embraced our holding in Strang so we do too. We therefore hold that a debt from money obtained by fraud in non-dischargeable in bankruptcy regardless of the debtor's culpability. The judgment of the Ninth Circuit is affirmed. The decision of the Court is unanimous.
